content.text: AWS charges you only for the resources you actually consume — compute seconds, gigabytes stored, and bytes transferred — with no upfront purchase and no minimum fee. The moment you stop using a resource, billing stops with it.

In traditional data centres you buy servers to handle peak load, which sit mostly idle during off-peak hours. With AWS pay-as-you-go, idle capacity costs nothing. The financial model shifts from provisioning for the future to paying for the present.

attrs.alt: AWS Pay-As-You-Go — three billing dimensions (compute time, storage volume, data transfer) flowing into the AWS billing engine
attrs.title: AWS Pay-As-You-Go Billing Dimensions

content.text: Almost every AWS service maps its usage to one or more of three fundamental dimensions. Understanding these dimensions lets you reason about cost across any service, even ones you have never used before.
Compute is billed for the duration a resource runs, measured in seconds, minutes, or request counts depending on the service.
per second (minimum 60 s) after the instance starts — a t3.micro costs ~$0.0104/hr in us-east-1.
$0.20 per million requests plus $0.0000166667 per GB-second of memory × duration.
per vCPU-hour and per GB-hour for the duration the task is in RUNNING state only.
per db-instance-hour while the database is in an available state, plus separate storage and I/O dimensions.
Storage is billed per GB stored per month, prorated to the second. The meter runs whether or not you access the data.
$0.023/GB-month for the first 50 TB, with tiered discounts above that.
$0.08/GB-month billed on the provisioned volume size, not bytes actually written. Snapshot storage is additional at $0.05/GB-month.
$0.004/GB-month — 83 % cheaper than Standard, but retrieval fees and minimum object size apply.
$0.25/GB-month plus separate on-demand read/write request unit charges.
Data transfer pricing is asymmetric — AWS charges for outbound data but makes inbound free, reflecting the direction that consumes internet transit capacity.
$0.09/GB for the first 10 TB/month (us-east-1); first 100 GB/month is free.
FREE — no charge for data entering AWS from the public internet.
$0.01/GB in each direction. This catches teams by surprise when microservices span AZs.
$0.0085/GB (HTTP) — cheaper than direct EC2 egress, and origin-to-CloudFront transfer is free.

AWS runs a metering pipeline that continuously samples resource usage across all services and regions, then assembles a monthly invoice. Understanding the timeline helps you diagnose billing surprises.
Each service emits usage records (instance-hours, GB-seconds, request counts) in near real time.
Usage is accumulated by account, service, region, and usage type on an hourly basis.
Tiered rates are applied (cheaper at higher volumes), then Savings Plan and Reserved Instance discounts reduce the total.
A final invoice is generated 3–5 business days after month-end. Usage data appears in Cost Explorer within 24 hours but is marked unblended until finalised.
You can query your current-month costs programmatically via the Cost Explorer API at any time during the month.

# Current-month cost by service
aws ce get-cost-and-usage \
  --time-period Start=$(date +%Y-%m-01),End=$(date +%Y-%m-%d) \
  --granularity MONTHLY \
  --metrics BlendedCost \
  --group-by Type=DIMENSION,Key=SERVICE \
  --query 'ResultsByTime[0].Groups[*].[Keys[0],Metrics.BlendedCost.Amount]' \
  --output table
AWS offers a free tier that lets you explore services at no cost. The three types have different expiry rules:
EC2 t2.micro 750 hrs/month; S3 5 GB Standard
Amazon Macie 30-day trial; AWS WAF 30-day trial

Free tier limits are per account, not per region, and they reset monthly (for recurring types). Exceeding any limit immediately incurs standard pay-as-you-go charges — AWS does not cap or stop usage automatically.

# Check free tier usage for the current month
aws ce get-dimension-values \
  --time-period Start=$(date +%Y-%m-01),End=$(date +%Y-%m-%d) \
  --dimension USAGE_TYPE

# Or view free tier alert status
aws budgets describe-budgets \
  --account-id $(aws sts get-caller-identity --query Account --output text)
EC2 instances are billed from the second they enter the
state until they are terminated (not stopped). Stopped instances still incur EBS storage charges for their root volume.
Per-second billing (minimum 60 seconds) means a batch job that runs for 90 seconds costs exactly 1.5 minutes of instance time — there is no rounding up to the next hour as there was before 2017.
Lambda has two independent pricing meters that are summed. Both have generous free tier allowances that cover most low-traffic functions permanently.
$0.20 per million invocations (first 1 M/month free always).
$0.0000166667 per GB-second — memory (GB) × execution time (seconds). First 400,000 GB-seconds/month free.
Optional pre-warming costs $0.000004646 per GB-second regardless of invocations. Only use it when cold-start latency is a measurable problem.

# Estimate Lambda monthly cost
# 1M invocations, 512 MB memory, avg 200 ms execution time

requests   = 1_000_000
memory_gb  = 0.512
duration_s = 0.200

request_cost  = max(0, requests - 1_000_000) / 1_000_000 * 0.20
gb_seconds    = memory_gb * duration_s * requests
duration_cost = max(0, gb_seconds - 400_000) * 0.0000166667

print(f"Request cost:  ${request_cost:.4f}")
print(f"Duration cost: ${duration_cost:.4f}")
print(f"Total:         ${request_cost + duration_cost:.4f}")
# Output: Total: $0.0000 — fully within the free tier at this volume
S3 bills on three separate meters: storage (per GB-month), requests (per 1,000 API calls), and data retrieval (for infrequent/archive tiers). Standard storage pricing uses volume tiers.

PUT/COPY/POST/LIST requests cost $0.005 per 1,000; GET/SELECT cost $0.0004 per 1,000. A workload that reads millions of small objects can accumulate significant request costs independent of storage volume.
RDS combines multiple billing meters that stack on each other. Failing to account for all of them is the most common cause of RDS cost surprises.
Charged while the instance is available, even with zero connections. Multi-AZ doubles this cost.
$0.115/GB-month for gp2, $0.10/GB-month for io1 plus $0.10 per provisioned IOPS.
For magnetic storage: $0.10 per million I/O requests (gp2 and gp3 have no per-I/O charge).
Free up to 100 % of database storage size; $0.095/GB-month beyond that.
Same egress rates as EC2; replication traffic between AZs in Multi-AZ is free.

Many AWS services apply lower rates as usage increases within a calendar month. Tiers reset at the start of each month, so monthly aggregation is the relevant window — not cumulative spend across months.
Tier discounts are applied automatically — you do not need to sign anything or negotiate. They apply to the aggregate within a single account (or an AWS Organisation using consolidated billing).
AWS provides tools to estimate prospective costs and understand historical spend. Use them in combination — no single tool answers every question.
Web-based tool to model a future architecture. Produces a monthly estimate you can share as a link or export to CSV.
Visualise and filter actual past spend. Offers a 12-month look-back and a 12-month forecast. Accessible from the billing console or via API.
Line-item hourly or daily export to S3 in CSV/Parquet. The most granular source for cost allocation and chargeback. Query with Athena.
Machine-readable price list for every service, region, and instance type. Useful for building internal cost models or tooling.

# Query current On-Demand price for a specific EC2 instance type
# The Pricing API is only available in us-east-1 and ap-south-1
aws pricing get-products \
  --service-code AmazonEC2 \
  --filters \
    "Type=TERM_MATCH,Field=instanceType,Value=m5.large" \
    "Type=TERM_MATCH,Field=location,Value=US East (N. Virginia)" \
    "Type=TERM_MATCH,Field=operatingSystem,Value=Linux" \
    "Type=TERM_MATCH,Field=tenancy,Value=Shared" \
    "Type=TERM_MATCH,Field=preInstalledSw,Value=NA" \
    "Type=TERM_MATCH,Field=capacitystatus,Value=Used" \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'PriceList[0]' --output text | python3 -m json.tool | grep -A2 'OnDemand'
Several AWS cost patterns routinely surprise teams that are new to pay-as-you-go billing. Knowing them in advance prevents mid-month billing shocks.
Data transfer between Availability Zones ($0.01/GB each direction):
Traffic between EC2 instances, or EC2 to RDS, in different AZs within the same region is not free. High-throughput microservices that span AZs can generate significant cross-AZ transfer costs.
EBS billed on provisioned size, not used bytes:
A 1 TB gp3 volume attached to a barely-used instance costs $80/month regardless of how much data is written to it.
$0.045/GB of data processed through a NAT Gateway. Private subnet instances that download large packages or stream data amplify this cost quickly.
The first snapshot copies all data; subsequent snapshots store only changes. Stale, forgotten snapshot chains can silently accumulate GB-months of cost.
Multi-AZ runs a synchronous standby in a second AZ. That standby instance is billed at full On-Demand rates even though it accepts no read traffic in standard Multi-AZ.
Basic monitoring (5-minute intervals) is free; detailed monitoring (1-minute intervals) adds a per-instance charge.
Enable a zero-dollar AWS Budget alert on every new account. This catches runaway resources before they become significant — the cost of the alert itself is negligible.

AWS Budgets lets you set spending or usage thresholds and receive SNS/email alerts when actual or forecasted costs cross a threshold. Unlike CloudWatch Billing Alarms, Budgets supports forecast-based alerting and can trigger automated actions.
Create a budget for the account, a specific service, a cost category, or a tag-filtered group.
Set alert thresholds at 80 %, 100 %, and optionally 120 % of the budget amount.
Configure SNS notification subscribers or email addresses for each threshold.
Optionally add Budget Actions: automatically apply an IAM policy to restrict provisioning, or stop EC2/RDS instances when the budget is breached.
Up to 62 budgets are free per account; additional budgets cost $0.02/day each. Budget Actions can invoke SSM Run Command or Step Functions automations for self-healing cost responses.

On-Demand is the baseline pay-as-you-go price. AWS offers commitment-based discounts that lower the per-unit cost in exchange for a 1- or 3-year pledge. The two models are used together — commit what you know is stable, pay on-demand for the rest.
Fixed instance family + AZ or region
The optimal strategy is a three-layer approach: Savings Plans or RIs for the stable baseline (committed), On-Demand for predictable but variable load, and Spot for fault-tolerant burst capacity.

Consider a typical three-tier web application in us-east-1 running on On-Demand pricing. Understanding how each component contributes to the monthly bill helps prioritise where commitment discounts will have the greatest impact.
20 GB stored, 500 GB egress via CF

Applying a 1-year Compute Savings Plan to the EC2 portion alone (at ~40 % discount) would reduce the bill by approximately $79/month. The RDS instance is a strong candidate for a 1-year Reserved Instance at ~42 % savings, saving a further $102/month.
A Lambda function that receives zero invocations costs zero. An EC2 instance stopped overnight costs only EBS storage.
Costs track workload automatically. A traffic spike that doubles the instance count doubles only the incremental compute cost, not the base cost of everything else.
New services can be tested at negligible cost. A proof-of-concept that runs for two hours costs two hours of instance time.
Costs rise with business volume and fall when business contracts. This natural coupling improves unit economics visibility.
Every charge traces to a specific resource, usage type, and time window. CUR + Athena lets you answer 'what generated this $47 line item' to the minute.
Runaway jobs, forgotten test resources, and traffic anomalies can generate unexpected charges. Budgets and Anomaly Detection are non-optional in production.
Higher per-unit cost than committed pricing:
On-Demand is the most expensive tier. Stable workloads paying On-Demand prices for months or years are leaving 40–70 % savings on the table.
Large architectures can have dozens of billing dimensions active simultaneously. Understanding your own bill requires dedicated FinOps tooling and practice.
NAT Gateway, cross-AZ transfer, and request-based charges can turn a small architectural choice into a significant monthly line item at scale.
Unlike a fixed CapEx purchase, there is no natural forcing function to retire unused resources. Tagging policies, Cost Explorer reports, and automated cleanup are needed disciplines.

Pay-as-you-go pricing converts your cloud provider into an on-demand utility — but unlike electricity, no breaker trips when you leave the lights on. The discipline of switching things off must come from your team.

AWS Cost Anomaly Detection uses machine learning to establish a normal spending baseline for each service, linked account, cost category, or cost allocation tag, then alerts you when actual spend deviates significantly. Unlike a fixed budget threshold, it adapts to your natural growth curve so you don't receive constant alerts as the business scales.
AWS Services (all services), Linked Account (per member account in an Org), Cost Category, or Cost Allocation Tag.
Individual alert (per anomaly detected) or daily/weekly digest. Individual alerts have the fastest response time for runaway cost events.
Set a minimum absolute dollar impact (e.g. only alert if the anomaly exceeds $50) to avoid noise from cheap services that spike to trivial amounts.
Each anomaly report identifies the service, usage type, and region that drove the spike, significantly reducing investigation time.

# Create an anomaly monitor for all AWS services
aws ce create-anomaly-monitor \
  --anomaly-monitor '{
    "MonitorName": "AllServices",
    "MonitorType": "DIMENSIONAL",
    "MonitorDimension": "SERVICE"
  }'

# Attach an alert subscription (SNS) to that monitor
MONITOR_ARN=$(aws ce get-anomaly-monitors \
  --query 'AnomalyMonitors[?MonitorName==`AllServices`].MonitorArn' \
  --output text)

aws ce create-anomaly-subscription \
  --anomaly-subscription '{
    "SubscriptionName": "DailyCostAlert",
    "MonitorArnList": ["'"$MONITOR_ARN"'"],
    "Subscribers": [{
      "Address": "arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:cost-alerts",
      "Type": "SNS"
    }],
    "Frequency": "DAILY",
    "Threshold": 50
  }'

Cost Anomaly Detection itself is free; you pay only for the SNS notifications it triggers. It typically learns a baseline within 10–14 days of enablement, after which its anomaly signals are significantly more useful than fixed-threshold budget alerts alone.

Tags are key-value pairs attached to AWS resources. When you activate a tag key as a Cost Allocation Tag in the Billing console, it appears as a filterable dimension in Cost Explorer, CUR, and Budgets. Without consistent tagging, you cannot answer 'which team, product, or environment generated this charge.'
Define a mandatory tag policy covering at least four keys — Environment, Team, Project, and CostCenter — applied consistently across all resource types.
Enforce tags with AWS Config rules or AWS Organizations Tag Policies. Untagged resources fail compliance checks and trigger remediation.
Activate each tag key as a Cost Allocation Tag in the Billing and Cost Management console (takes up to 24 hours to appear in Cost Explorer).
Create per-team or per-environment Budgets filtered by the activated tag to enable team-level chargeback or showback.
Audit untagged resources monthly using Cost Explorer's 'Tag coverage' report and tag older resources retroactively.

# Apply cost allocation tags to an EC2 instance
aws ec2 create-tags \
  --resources i-0abc123def456 \
  --tags \
    Key=Environment,Value=production \
    Key=Team,Value=platform \
    Key=Project,Value=checkout-api \
    Key=CostCenter,Value=CC-1042

# Check tag coverage via Cost Explorer
aws ce get-tags \
  --time-period Start=$(date +%Y-%m-01),End=$(date +%Y-%m-%d) \
  --tag-key Team \
  --query 'Tags' --output table
Rightsizing: Pay Less by Running Less

Pay-as-you-go pricing means the cheapest way to reduce a bill is to use fewer or smaller resources. AWS Compute Optimizer and Cost Explorer Rightsizing Recommendations analyse CloudWatch utilisation metrics over 14 days and identify over-provisioned instances.
Provides ML-based recommendations for EC2, EBS, Lambda, ECS Fargate, and Auto Scaling groups. Identifies instances running at < 40 % CPU as over-provisioned.
Automatically starts and stops instances on a schedule. A development server running 8 hours/day instead of 24 reduces its compute cost by 67 %.
Automatically moves objects between access tiers based on access frequency. Objects not accessed for 30 consecutive days move to the Infrequent Access tier at no retrieval cost — unlike Standard-IA.

# Get Compute Optimizer recommendations for EC2
aws compute-optimizer get-ec2-instance-recommendations \
  --query 'instanceRecommendations[*].{
    Instance: instanceArn,
    Finding: finding,
    CurrentType: currentInstanceType,
    RecommendedType: recommendationOptions[0].instanceType,
    MonthlySavings: recommendationOptions[0].estimatedMonthlySavings.value
  }' \
  --output table

Rightsizing compound with commitment discounts: downsizing an instance from m5.xlarge to m5.large first, then buying a Savings Plan on the smaller instance, achieves greater total savings than buying a commitment on the over-provisioned instance.
AWS measures and bills three fundamental dimensions: compute time, storage volume, and data transfer — understanding these lets you reason about cost for any service.
The free tier has three types (Always Free, 12-Month, Trial) with different expiry rules; exceeding any limit immediately incurs standard charges.
Volume tiers automatically apply lower rates as monthly consumption grows; consolidated billing across an AWS Organisation pools usage toward tier thresholds.
On-Demand is the most flexible but most expensive pricing tier; Savings Plans and Reserved Instances offer 40–72 % discounts for committed baselines.
AWS Budgets, Cost Explorer, and the Cost and Usage Report are the three essential tools for controlling and understanding pay-as-you-go spend.
